headline: CATTLE RALLY FROM $250 FLOOR DESPITE OIL CRASH
subheadline: Live cattle gained 0.4% to $252.93 while crude oil crashed 6% overnight.
lead: Live cattle held the $250 floor and bounced to $252.93, up 0.4%, while crude oil crashed 6% overnight to $94.54. The cattle complex is acting like Monday's breakdown was a flush, not a structural break — feeders jumped 1.4% to $372.23 and the funds aren't adding short pressure. Oil's collapse removes input cost pressure but the cattle story is now about whether this bounce has legs or runs into the same selling that's been capping rallies since March.
the_takeaway: Cattle bounce is real but needs follow-through above $255 to matter.
teaser: Live cattle found buyers at $250 while oil crashed 6% overnight.

one_number.context: Feeders jumped 1.4% today, the strongest gain in three weeks. When feeders outpace live cattle this sharply, it usually means the market thinks the worst of the cattle selloff is over. The feeder premium to live cattle compressed to 47% today from 49% Monday — still wide by historical standards but moving the right direction for cattle bulls.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's call was that the cattle bounce was real but thin, with feeder weakness keeping the breakdown thesis alive.
yesterdays_call.note: Feeders led today's rally with a 1.4% gain, the opposite of yesterday's weakness.

sections.title: Cattle Rally From Floor
sections.body: Live cattle bounced 0.4% to $252.93 after finding support right at the $250 level that's been the complex's floor since February. Feeders led the rally with a 1.4% gain to $372.23, the strongest single-day performance in three weeks. The feeder-to-live ratio compressed to 1.47 from Monday's 1.49, signaling the market thinks the worst of the cattle selloff is behind it. Cash cattle trade hasn't reset yet but the $252.93 close puts the futures back above the 50-day moving average at $251.80. The question now is whether this bounce finds follow-through or runs into the same overhead resistance that's capped rallies since March.
sections.bottom_line: Floor held, feeders led, overhead resistance still the test.
sections.vs_yesterday: Feeders: complete reversal from yesterday's weakness to today's leadership.

sections.title: Oil Crashes Six Percent
sections.body: WTI crude crashed 6% overnight to $94.54, the biggest single-day drop since January. No specific catalyst drove the selloff but the technical damage is real — crude broke below the 200-day moving average at $96.20 and closed at the lowest level in six weeks. For ag operators, cheaper crude means lower diesel and fertilizer input costs heading into peak planting season. Natural gas also weakened, down 1.7% to $2.73, keeping nitrogen costs under pressure. The energy complex's weakness removes one headwind for grain margins but the impact won't show up in basis levels for another week.
sections.bottom_line: Input cost relief arrives just as planting hits peak season.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor diesel basis — retail prices lag futures by 7-10 days.

sections.title: Grains Ease on Fund Flow
sections.body: Corn dropped 1.1% to $4.72 while soybeans eased just 0.3% to $12.06, the divergence telling you fund money is still rotating out of corn into beans. December corn fell harder than July, widening the carry to 21 cents and confirming the market doesn't see a weather premium developing yet. Chicago wheat led the grain complex lower, down 1.8% to $6.13, as managed money continues liquidating long positions ahead of harvest. Planting progress hits 42% complete for corn and 18% for soybeans, both slightly ahead of the five-year average. The seasonal says grain volatility should pick up as we approach prevent plant deadlines, but today's action suggests the funds are more interested in unwinding positions than adding weather risk.
sections.bottom_line: Fund flow drives grain action more than weather concerns right now.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: When this ratio compresses, it usually means the market expects cattle feeding margins to improve. Today's move from 1.49 to 1.47 is the biggest single-day compression in three weeks, suggesting the worst of the cattle complex selloff may be over. Watch for the ratio to test 1.45 — that's where it's topped out during previous cattle rallies this year.

basis.headline: Corn basis steady as ethanol margins hold firm
basis.body: Eastern Corn Belt basis is holding steady despite futures weakness as ethanol margins remain profitable above $0.30 per gallon. Western Belt continues to lag with rail transportation costs elevated. Soybean basis is showing early signs of tightening in the Delta as river levels support barge rates. Wheat basis continues to weaken ahead of harvest pressure.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex find support at $250 or continue the breakdown through key technical levels?
weekly_thread.status_text: The $250 floor held and cattle bounced to $252.93 with feeders leading the rally. The support test is passing so far.

the_more_you_know.title: The Feeder-Live Ratio: When Margins Talk
the_more_you_know.body: Today's feeder-to-live cattle ratio compressed to 1.47 from Monday's 1.49, the biggest single-day move in three weeks. This ratio is the market's real-time read on cattle feeding margins — when it compresses (feeders gain less than live cattle), it signals improving economics for cattle feeders. The 1.47 level puts the ratio back in the range it held from February through April, suggesting the cattle complex's recent weakness was more about technical selling than fundamental deterioration. Historical patterns show that when this ratio compresses below 1.45, cattle rallies tend to have staying power. Above 1.50, the complex usually struggles to maintain upward momentum.
